# Interrogator Forensic Dossier — SoftwareOne Holding AG (SWON.SW)

*Sector: Technology (IT services / software & cloud reseller) · Signal: deep_value · Transcript: FY2025 results call, 2026-03-31 (single call; combined SoftwareOne + 6 months Crayon)*

> **Evidence note:** Only ONE transcript was provided (the FY2025 results call held 2026-03-31), not the usual up-to-8-quarter sequence. The "narrative arc / tone trajectory across quarters" sections are therefore reconstructed from (a) the multi-year financial trajectory in the metrics block, (b) the internal evolution *within* this call (FY recap → Q4 inflection → 2026 outlook), (c) the documented corporate history (2023–24 Bain take-private saga; Dec-2024 Crayon announcement; July-2025 Crayon close), and (d) live-verified post-transcript events (Q1-2026 beat-and-raise reported 2026-05-12; Capital Markets Day 2030 targets, 2026-06-09). Cross-quarter quotes are not fabricated where absent.

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The SoftwareOne story has been through three distinct regimes, and the FY2025 call sits squarely at the hinge of the third.

- **Regime 1 (2021–2022) — "growth reseller, then a stumble."** The 2021 print (rev CHF 914M, NI CHF 118M, EPS 0.76) was the high-water mark of clean profitability. 2022 cratered to a CHF -58M net loss (EPS -0.38). This is the period that bred the 2023 Bain Capital take-private approach (CHF 18.50→20.50/sh, ultimately rejected) and the founder/board war.
- **Regime 2 (2023–2024) — "independent, but stalling, and digesting Microsoft."** Revenue flat-lined (1.01B → 1.02B), net income oscillated around zero (CHF +21M → CHF -2M), EPS collapsed to break-even. The core franchise was being squeezed by **Microsoft incentive changes on Enterprise Agreements (EA)** — the recurring villain of this whole transcript. Management's answer was the **December 2024 agreed combination with Crayon** (NOK 69 cash + 0.8233 SWON share/Crayon share), closed July 2025 with a compulsory squeeze-out of the remainder.
- **Regime 3 (2025–onward) — "global software & cloud leader, scale + synergies + AI."** This call is the first full-year framing of the merged entity. The narrative pivots hard to **scale** ("CHF 14bn combined gross sales… 70,000 clients… 13,000 colleagues… one of a kind"), **synergies** (CHF 43M run-rate at FY-end, CHF 64M "as of today," target CHF 80–100M), and a **Q4 inflection** ("by Q3 we were back to positive territory… in Q4 we reached 11% revenue growth"). New growth drivers introduced: **channel** (first global authorized distributor for Google Cloud, Feb-2026), **CSP transition** offsetting EA headwinds, and **AI** (Anthropic/OpenAI reseller-partner opportunity).

What was quietly dropped: the standalone "we'll create more value than Bain offered" framing of 2024. The thesis has been re-platformed onto Crayon scale rather than organic SoftwareOne re-acceleration. Two growth drivers were also quietly *de-scoped*: **North America** (−12.6% in 2025, "GTM-related sales execution challenges") and **LATAM** (−4.4%, plus a decision to **exit 4 non-strategic countries** — Argentina, Uruguay, El Salvador, Nicaragua — and a CHF 8M LATAM impairment).

The arc has since extended (live-verified, post-transcript): the inflection management promised **continued** — Q1-2026 (reported 2026-05-12) beat and the FY2026 revenue guide was **raised** to mid-to-high single digit; and at the **June-9-2026 Capital Markets Day** the board set new 2030 targets (HSD revenue CAGR, EBITDA margin >28%, FCF conversion >60%) and a **30–50% payout dividend policy**. The thesis the call advertised has, so far, been delivered.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim (FY25 call) | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| "2025 was transformational… returned to growth, +1.4% LFL, accelerating to **11% in Q4**" | Screener: rev +22.5% YoY (1.02B→1.24B) — but that is *reported*, inflated by 6 months of Crayon. **LFL +1.4%** is the honest organic figure. Q4 +11% LFL is real but is a single quarter; Q1-2026 +67.4% reported beat subsequently corroborated continuation. | **Mostly supported, but the headline reported growth is M&A optics, not organic.** The 22.5% is acquisition arithmetic. |
| "Profitability remained strong, **adj. EBITDA margin 20.9%**, +0.5pp YoY" | Screener net margin **0.1%**; FY2025 NI **CHF 0.9M** (EPS 0.0059). TTM diluted EPS **−0.01**. | **Adjusted ≠ reported, and the gap is enormous.** A 20.9% *adjusted* EBITDA margin coexists with a ~zero net margin — the bridge is amortization of Crayon intangibles, integration/acquisition costs, and the "below CHF 30M adjustments (ex-Crayon)" they're proud of. The director must treat "20.9%" as a normalization claim, not GAAP reality. |
| "Good progress on synergies — CHF 43M run-rate FY-end, CHF 64M today, target 80–100M" | Not independently verifiable from this transcript, but FCF corroborates direction: **TTM FCF CHF 228M; FCF margin 16.3%; FCF +702% YoY**; P/FCF **5.4x**. | **Corroborated by cash flow.** The synergy story shows up where it should — in FCF, which is the genuinely impressive line. ROIC 10.7% is respectable. |
| "No headwinds from EA incentives in 2026… if anything, stabilization" | EA changes were *the* drag across 2023–25. EPS 5-yr CAGR **−70.3%** is the scar. | **Plausible, and being borne out.** This was the most important forward claim; the Q1-2026 beat-and-raise is the first hard evidence it held. Microsoft-incentive risk remains structural and recurring. |
| "No further impairments expected in 2026 (only LATAM CHF 8M in 2025)" | Altman-Z **0.6** (distress zone — but mis-calibrated for a net-cash, asset-light reseller), Piotroski **3/9** (weak). Goodwill from Crayon is large and fresh. | **Watch closely.** Management explicitly tied "no impairment" to "current business plans"; any region missing plan (NA, LATAM) re-opens goodwill risk. |
| Balance-sheet strength implied | **NET CASH CHF 360M** (cash 434M vs debt 74M; live-corrected). Net debt screen figure −403M directionally agrees. | **Strongly supported — this is the load-bearing fact of the value case.** Real, un-levered, post-Crayon-cash net cash. |

The story is **richer than the GAAP P&L but poorer than the reported revenue line suggests.** The truth lives in the cash flow statement (FCF, net cash), not in either the headline +22.5% revenue (M&A optics) or the 0.1% net margin (amortization/integration noise).

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING (within-call and vs the 2023–24 defensive crouch).**

- **Opening tone — offensive/confident:** "2025 was *transformational*," "we have *delivered*," "the actions we are taking *are working*." This is materially more forward-leaning than the defensive, Bain-besieged posture of 2023–24.
- **On the EA headwind (the historical pain point) — newly dismissive in a good way:** "we do not see any headwinds… related to the EA incentives. If anything, there will be stabilization" (Mulholland). The single biggest multi-year drag is being declared over — and the subsequent Q1 beat began to validate it.
- **On margins — disciplined, almost cautious:** Schraner repeatedly anchors "below CHF 30M adjustments," "narrow the gap between reported and adjusted," "similar capex level." This is the right *defensive* counterweight to Mulholland's offense — a CFO managing the credibility gap.
- **On weak regions — candid, not evasive:** "2025 was a *disappointing* year in North America… −12.6%"; explicit country exits in LATAM. Naming losses plainly is a green flag for credibility. Schraner set a falsifiable near-mark — "Q1 better than Q4" — and then cleared it.

Net: management has shifted from *defensive* (2023–24: cost, macro, "we're undervalued") to *offensive* (2025: scale, synergies, AI, channel) — but with a CFO keeping the offense honest. That blend is the most credible configuration.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH (improving; the operational track now has two consecutive delivered prints, though the bottom-line track record is still thin).**

- **The one delivered beat in-call:** "we delivered 1.4% revenue growth… *ahead of our initial expectation of broadly flat*." A modest, verifiable over-delivery on the LFL line.
- **Synergy guide is being *raised* in real time:** FY-end CHF 43M → CHF 64M "as of today" → 80–100M target. Front-loaded delivery is the single best credibility signal in the call.
- **Post-transcript confirmation (live-verified):** Q1-2026 (2026-05-12) **beat** consensus (CHF 387.7M vs 384.5M) and management **raised** the FY2026 revenue guide; CFO's "Q1 better than Q4" mark was kept. The operational guide-to-delivery loop is now corroborated twice.
- **But the screener flags a brutal historical guidance record:** **EPS beat 0/7 quarters (0%)**. Whatever the *adjusted* narrative, the company has not beaten *bottom-line* expectations in nearly two years. EPS 5-yr CAGR −70.3%. This is the credibility anchor the director cannot wave away — the bottom line remains hostage to integration adjustments.
- **Forward consensus is optimistic:** fwd EPS growth +28.7%, latest-Q EPS YoY +26%. The Street is buying the inflection.

So: the *operational/synergy* guidance is being delivered or beaten; the *EPS* guidance has been missed serially. The reconciliation is that 2023–25 EPS misses were the Microsoft-EA + integration-cost era, and management is arguing — with the first two prints of evidence — that era is ending.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

Analysts pushed hardest on exactly the right risks:

- **Microsoft EA incentives (Treisch, Kepler):** "have you actually delivered better than expected… how much of a tailwind can it become?" Management answered "delivered better than expected" and "no headwind in 2026" — confident, but this is the recurring single-factor dependency.
- **North America recovery timing (Burgi, F&W):** "can we expect that in the second half… be more precise." Schraner gave a *soft* answer — "return to growth in 2026," "Q1 better than Q4," no hard date. The softest point in the call at the time — **subsequently partially vindicated** by the Q1 beat.
- **Further impairments (Wolf, Berenberg):** directly asked if 2026 could see more write-downs. Schraner gave a conditional "no… based on what we know today." Conditional language on goodwill is a flag given the fresh Crayon goodwill.
- **Exceptionals/adjustments cadence (Tong, UBS):** "anything else we should be mindful of?" — the analyst is implicitly worried the "below CHF 30M" promise excludes Crayon integration cost, which Schraner confirmed: *"This does not include the Crayon acquisition cost or cost related to the integration."* That carve-out is where reported EPS keeps leaking.
- **AI (Wolf):** how OpenAI/Anthropic treat resellers. Mulholland's answer was opportunity-framed but vague on monetization — aspirational, not yet in the numbers.

The pattern: management is crisp on synergies and EA, **soft on NA timing and on what the adjustment carve-outs really cost.**

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Red flags**
- **EPS beat 0/7 quarters; EPS 5-yr CAGR −70.3%.** Bottom-line credibility is broken until proven otherwise.
- **Altman-Z 0.6 / Piotroski 3/9** — quantitatively distressed *scores* despite a net-cash balance sheet; Altman-Z is mis-calibrated for an asset-light, net-cash reseller, but the thin margins and fresh goodwill it reflects are real.
- **"Adjusted" doing heavy lifting:** 20.9% adj. EBITDA vs ~0% net margin; Crayon integration costs explicitly carved out of the "below CHF 30M" adjustment promise.
- **North America −12.6%** with only soft, internal-metric evidence of recovery at call time; LATAM −4.4% + 4 country exits + impairment.
- **Reported +22.5% revenue is M&A optics**, not organic (organic LFL +1.4%).
- **Microsoft single-vendor dependency** remains the master risk; the whole "no 2026 headwind" thesis rests on Microsoft's incentive posture.

🟢 **Green flags**
- **NET CASH CHF 360M** — genuine, post-merger, a hard SoP add-back (~CHF 2.3/share).
- **TTM FCF CHF 228M, P/FCF 5.4x, FCF +702% YoY, 16.3% FCF margin** — the cash engine is real and cheap.
- **Q4 LFL +11%; CSP transition offsetting the EA loss** — the inflection is in the data, then confirmed by the Q1-2026 beat-and-raise.
- **Synergies front-loaded and being raised** (43→64→target 100M) — best credibility signal in the call.
- **Google Cloud global authorized distributor (Feb-2026)** + **channel +18.7%** — a concrete, dated new growth leg.
- **Candid disclosure** on NA/LATAM losses and impairments — no whitewashing.
- **CMD-2026 (June 9) capital-return turn:** new 2030 targets (EBITDA margin >28%, FCF conversion >60%) + a **30–50% dividend policy** — the board now believes the cash is durable enough to distribute.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Segment re-cut is a tell:** "rest of Europe" was restructured into **Nordics / Western Europe / CEE** post-Crayon. Re-segmentation right after a merger both reflects real integration *and* resets the comparison base — harder for analysts to track organic trends for a couple of quarters. Watch for the LFL bridge to get murkier before it gets clearer.
- **New voices, new emphasis:** the call is led by **co-COOs Mulholland & Erb + CFO Schraner** — a Crayon-integrated leadership team. Mulholland (offense: scale/AI/synergy) and Schraner (defense: adjustments/capex discipline) play distinct, complementary roles. This is a more institutional cadence than the founder-dominated 2023 era.
- **AI framed as "complexity = our friend":** "our business model really thrives around complexity… we help guide customers around which model makes sense." Reseller/advisory positioning on AI is credible *direction* but **zero quantified contribution** — it's optionality, not yet earnings.
- **"Microsoft price increases help our business"** (Mulholland) — a structural tell: the franchise monetizes vendor price inflation. Underappreciated tailwind if Microsoft keeps raising.
- **Philippines public-sector receivable** ($22M collected, rest "shortly") — a small but real Crayon legacy clean-up; closing it removes a working-capital overhang.
- **The collapsed Bain/Apax take-private (2023–24) is the silent backdrop — and it is now CLOSED, not dormant:** PE repeatedly wanted this at CHF 18.50–20.50/sh and ~CHF 2.9–3.2bn. The shareholder-group/Bain agreement was *dissolved* (March-2024) and the company chose the Crayon path and to stay public. The take-out optionality that hung over the name for two years has been *spent*; do not underwrite a fresh bid.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STRENGTHENING (post-pivot, integration trough passed).** SoftwareOne has pivoted from a stalled standalone reseller into a scaled, Crayon-merged software/cloud platform, and the FY2025 call was the first print where the pivot showed real traction (Q4 +11% LFL, FCF +702%, synergies front-loaded, EA headwind declared over) — now corroborated by the Q1-2026 beat-and-raise and the June-9 CMD. The balance sheet (net cash CHF 360M) and the cash engine (P/FCF 5.4x) are unambiguously strong and cheap.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *the catalysts are SPENT, not pending.* The three load-bearing events — the Crayon merger, the Bain/Apax take-private, and the FY2025-beat → CMD re-rating — have **all already fired**: Crayon closed (squeeze-out July-2025); the PE buyout was rejected/dissolved (2024) and the company is committed to staying public; and the strong Q1-2026 beat-and-raise (May-12) plus the CMD (June-9) are in the rear-view, with the stock already re-rated ~10% off them into the ~CHF 8 print. So the real question becomes the *value* question, not the *event* question: is the "adjusted" inflection real, durable GAAP earnings power, or is it permanently masked by Crayon integration costs, intangible amortization, and the 0/7 EPS-beat history? The value case rests entirely on FCF + net cash being trustworthy while the P&L is noise — and on the synergy/margin bridge (>23% in 2026, >28% by 2030) actually landing in cash. If integration costs run hotter/longer than guided (Schraner's Crayon-cost carve-out is the leak point) or NA/LATAM miss plan and re-open goodwill, the cheap-on-FCF thesis converges slowly. Size this as a **deep-value, synergy-realization compounder — NOT an event-driven special-situation** — and explicitly discount any thesis hinging on a fresh takeover or a catalyst re-rate that has already happened.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING

bull_thesis: SoftwareOne (SWON.SW) is an IT software-and-cloud reseller trading at about 5.5 times free cash flow (price divided by the cash the business generates after capital spending), with a net-cash balance sheet of roughly CHF 360m (CHF 434m of cash against CHF 74m of debt). It is mid-way through a real, executing merger with Crayon. The rough patch in 2024-25 was self-inflicted - a reset in Microsoft's reseller incentive program plus a stumble in the US go-to-market - rather than a structural loss of demand: revenue turned back up to +11% in the fourth quarter of 2025, and the adjusted profit margin (operating earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization, a measure of underlying cash profitability) reached 20.9%, up 0.5 percentage points. The Crayon combination, which closed on 2 July 2025, built a scale leader with CHF 14bn in combined gross sales, 70,000 clients, and a presence in 70 countries. The cost-savings engine is delivering ahead of plan: a CHF 64m annual run-rate at the March 2026 call against a CHF 80-100m target, with CHF 80m-plus already realized by the first quarter of 2026 and a CHF 100m run-rate targeted for end-2026. That savings bridge is what should lift the 20.9% adjusted margin toward the above-23% level management raised its 2026 guidance to at the first-quarter-2026 call (alongside mid-to-high-single-digit revenue growth at constant currency). The cash generation is real and under-appreciated: free cash flow over the trailing twelve months was CHF 228m, up about 702% year over year, a 16.3% free-cash-flow margin. New growth lanes are genuine - SoftwareOne became the first global authorized Google Cloud distributor in February 2026 across 10 markets, the shift to the cloud-solution-provider model is offsetting the Microsoft incentive drag, and an artificial-intelligence advisory role (cost management, migration, plus an explicit role reselling and helping clients choose between Anthropic and OpenAI models) makes generative AI a service tailwind rather than a threat. Valuation models agree on the upside: a discounted-cash-flow estimate points to about +23% (fair value CHF 10.8), and an earnings-power-value estimate (the worth of current earnings with no growth credit) to +58%. Against higher-priced listed peers - Softcat at roughly 5-6% net margin, Computacenter at roughly 3-4% - SoftwareOne at 5.5 times free cash flow, with a narrow but stable competitive edge from its breadth of vendor relationships and a clean balance sheet, is the cheap, mis-rated name of the group. What has to hold for the case to work: the cost savings land and close the gap between reported (IFRS accounting) margins and adjusted margins; North America (down 12.6% in 2025) and Latin America return to growth; there is no fresh write-down of the value assigned to Crayon in the purchase accounting; and the Microsoft incentive headwind stays behind them.
bear_thesis: The apparent cheapness hides a structurally low-quality reseller whose 'profit' exists only on the adjusted line. On a reported (IFRS accounting) basis the net margin is 0.1% - net income of CHF 0.9m on CHF 1.24bn of revenue - earnings per share of CHF 0.0059, and a five-year earnings-per-share growth rate of -70.3% (net income fell from CHF 118m in 2021 to a CHF 58m loss in 2022, then ground along near breakeven). The 20.9% adjusted operating margin is essentially a storytelling device: the chief financial officer's own stated goal is merely to 'narrow the gap' to under CHF 30m of adjustments, and that cap explicitly excludes Crayon integration costs - the single largest cash leakage is carved out of the number. Quality measures confirm the stress: an Altman Z-score of 0.6 (bankruptcy-risk territory), a Piotroski financial-strength score of 3 out of 9, return on equity of 1.8%, and a gross margin of 44.1% that is contracting. The company has beaten earnings expectations in 0 of the last 7 quarters (0%) - so a management team asserting the Microsoft incentive headwind is cleanly behind it in 2026 has earned skepticism, because Microsoft controls the incentive pool and can move the reseller's cut at will (the opposite of pricing power). The headline +22.5% revenue growth is merger arithmetic (six months of Crayon being added in); the honest organic figure is the +1.4% like-for-like growth management itself cites. The value assigned to Crayon in purchase accounting is a live write-down risk if North America and Latin America stay weak (only Latin America was written down, by CHF 8m, in 2025; the finance chief's 'no further impairments' promise is hostage to regional performance). The smart-money score is 9 out of 100 and institutional ownership flow is 50 out of 100 - no professional conviction. Several valuation models say it is not cheap once quality is accounted for: the Graham margin of safety is -100%, owner-earnings -54%, and an intrinsic-value-at-15-times estimate -100%. The catalyst - the Crayon merger - has already happened. It closed mid-2025, so there is no event-driven asymmetry left, only operational execution that has historically disappointed (0 of 7). The old Bain takeover bid (CHF 18.80-20.50) is dead (cancelled in January 2024), and anchoring to it is stale. What would confirm the bear case: a 2026 earnings miss, a fresh write-down of Crayon goodwill, or continued gross-margin contraction - any of which would expose the gap between adjusted and reported earnings as permanent, making 5.5 times free cash flow a value trap on a base where the reseller's cut is being squeezed.
consensus_delta: This is a deep-value case, so the question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company with stabilizing, net-cash-backed earning power. Partly. The assumption the market gets wrong is reading the roughly 0% reported net margin and earnings per share of CHF 0.0059 as the steady state of a broken reseller, when in fact (a) it is depressed by amortization of the Crayon purchase price and integration charges that will roll off, and (b) trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is CHF 228m, up about 702% year over year, at a 16.3% margin - so the cash earning power is an order of magnitude better than the accounting headline. The market also under-credits the savings delivery: a CHF 64m run-rate at the call, CHF 80m-plus by the first quarter of 2026, and a CHF 100m target for end-2026, which mechanically lifts the adjusted operating margin from 20.9% toward the above-23% level management raised guidance to. The smart-money score of 9 out of 100 confirms professionals are absent. Where the market is right (the other side of the disagreement): half the valuation models - the Graham margin of safety at -100%, owner-earnings at -54%, and an intrinsic-value-at-15-times estimate at -100% - flag that, once quality is accounted for, this is not cheap, and the 0-of-7 earnings-beat record means the market is reasonably demanding proof before paying up. The mispricing is real, but it is a 'prove it to me' re-rating, not a hidden-asset one.
forcing_function: The re-rating driver here is operational, not date-stamped, because the deal catalyst has already happened. What forces a re-rating: (1) the next full-year or half-year results showing the gap between reported and adjusted margins closing - the first clean combined-organic comparison and a reported operating-earnings line converging toward the 20.9%-to-23% adjusted path (the 2026 figures, with the next major update in the second half of 2026 / fourth quarter); (2) the CHF 100m savings run-rate being hit by end-2026 (management's own dated milestone, and the closest thing to a hard trigger); (3) a return of capital to shareholders - with CHF 360m of net cash and integration winding down, a buyback or dividend would force value investors to re-rate the stock, though none has been signaled, so this is possible rather than promised; and (4) a renewed strategic review or take-private approach - the company has been bid for before (Bain, which died in January 2024), and at 5.5 times free cash flow with net cash it is a plausible private-equity target again, but that is unverified today and not something to underwrite.
valley_of_death: The risky window over the next three to nine months, before the value can converge: (1) 2026 earnings - with a 0-of-7 beat history, a miss in the second quarter or first half of 2026, or a walk-back on the above-23% margin target, would re-confirm the bear case and punish the stock; (2) a fresh write-down of the Crayon purchase-price value - only Latin America (CHF 8m) was written down in 2025, so continued weakness in North America (down 12.6%) and Latin America (down 4.4%) could force a charge the finance chief has publicly said will not happen, a credibility hit; (3) integration-cost leakage - the chief financial officer's under-CHF 30m adjustments cap excludes Crayon costs, so reported operating earnings can keep lagging adjusted into the first half of 2026; and (4) a renewed tightening of Microsoft's incentive program - management asserts the headwind is behind them, but if Microsoft moves the reseller's cut again the thesis cracks at its source. Crucially, there is no looming debt maturity or cash-burn problem - the CHF 360m net-cash position means the balance sheet survives the wait; the danger here is an earnings disappointment, not insolvency.
positioning_washout: There is only a limited forced-selling dynamic at work. This is not an income stock (there is no dividend signal; the balance sheet is committed to integration), so there is no base of income investors that would be forced to sell on a dividend cut. The shareholder base already cleared out through the failed Bain saga of 2023-24 and the incentive-program air-pocket of 2024-25 (the smart-money score is 9 out of 100 and institutional flow is a neutral 50 out of 100 - professionals are largely gone). The remaining holders are deep-value and special-situation funds plus index trackers. The constructive read: with no crowded base of long holders, there is little forced selling left to fear - but equally there is no captive holder being shaken out to create an entry point. The wash-out is mostly behind the stock, which is why the practical downside is shallow (the net-cash floor) but a fresh capitulation flush would require a new negative catalyst (an earnings miss or a write-down), not mechanical de-positioning.
moderator_conclusion: SoftwareOne (SWON.SW) is a genuine deep-value name whose key catalyst has already happened - the Crayon merger closed on 2 July 2025, so the re-rating event itself is spent. What remains is operational execution of the cost savings (running ahead of plan: a CHF 64m run-rate rising to CHF 80m-plus, with a CHF 100m target for end-2026) and a raised 2026 outlook (mid-to-high-single-digit constant-currency growth, adjusted operating margin above 23%). Under the current market backdrop, the framework rewards hard-dated catalysts that fall in the favorable window, and structural special situations in thinly-covered distressed or deleveraging corners, while it penalizes catalysts that have already fired or fall outside that window. A completed merger-integration story sits in the penalized bucket, with no dated asymmetry left to credit, which caps how much conviction the catalyst can earn. That is why the call is to keep this on the watchlist for a capitulation rather than buy aggressively, at a moderate conviction of 3: the thesis is real, but the event is behind it, and the 0-of-7 earnings-beat history argues for waiting for a clean print showing reported margins converging upward, or a fresh wash-out (toward roughly CHF 7), before sizing up. Valuing each part of the business separately points to a base case of about CHF 13.5 (a bullish, savings-complete view of about CHF 17.5 and a bearish, distressed-multiple-on-today view of about CHF 10.5), in line with the discounted-cash-flow estimate of CHF 10.8 and the earnings-power-value estimate of about CHF 13, and well floored by the CHF 360m of net cash (about CHF 2.4 per share). That gives roughly +60% upside to the base case from about CHF 8.2-8.7, with downside protected by net cash. Separately, the value conviction is set at 4 - deliberately above the overall conviction - because, judged purely on valuation versus the sum of the parts plus forensic quality, and setting aside the spent catalyst and the timing penalty, this is a cheaper, better-quality value case than the catalyst score alone implies: net cash, real trailing free cash flow (CHF 228m, a 16.3% margin), 5.5 times free cash flow, roughly 60-110% upside to the sum-of-the-parts, and an earnings-power-value margin of safety of +58%. The competitive edge is narrow (breadth of vendor relationships, 70,000 client relationships, and the new global Google Cloud distributor authorization - but Microsoft controls the reseller's cut and gross margin is contracting) and stable (Crayon's scale offsets the squeeze on that cut). The longer-term threat (cheaper labor and generative AI displacing IT-services and software brokerage) is manageable rather than fatal - the multi-vendor complexity-brokerage mix, plus cloud-provider transition, cost management, migration, and AI-model selection, is harder to bypass than low-switching-cost software, and AI is currently a tailwind to services. Because the edge is narrow but stable (not eroding) and the secular threat is manageable (not terminal), no cap on the value conviction is triggered. In short: a moderate call on catalyst timing, a stronger one on intrinsic value - own it on a wash-out, not at today's bid.
sop_breakdown: The base case reconciles the bull view (about CHF 17.5, savings-complete plus a peer-discounted multiple) and the bear view (about CHF 10.5, a distressed multiple on today's accounting-thin earnings). It rests on: the operating reseller business at about CHF 11.1 per share (enterprise value of roughly CHF 1.7bn at about 7 times partially-realized adjusted operating earnings of about CHF 290m, between the trailing CHF 250m and the 2026 target of CHF 335m) plus the net-cash add-back of about CHF 2.4 per share, for about CHF 13.5 per share. Cross-checks: a discounted-cash-flow fair value of CHF 10.8 (a model floor) and an earnings-power-value margin of safety of +58% (about CHF 13.0) - the sum-of-the-parts sits in line with the models and well below the savings-complete ceiling. The net cash (CHF 360m, about CHF 2.4 per share) is the hard, verifiable component; the operating multiple carries the uncertainty.
risk_reward: The risk-reward is favorable from the screener price of about CHF 8.24 (the live price is about CHF 8.4-8.7). The downside to the bear estimate (a sum-of-the-parts of about CHF 10.5) actually sits above the current price - the net-cash and low-multiple floor is already above the market, which is the deep-value tell - so the practical downside is to a wash-out around CHF 7 (-15%), cushioned by the CHF 360m of net cash. The upside to the base fair value (CHF 13.5) is about +60%, and to the savings-complete case (CHF 17.5) about +110%. The asymmetry is genuinely positive (about 3-to-1 or 4-to-1 to the base case on a true wash-out entry), but it is paced by operations rather than gated by an event, because the catalyst has already fired.
peer_comps_note: On a peer basis the verdict is cheap relative to peers: SoftwareOne at 5.5 times free cash flow with a 44% gross margin is cheap in absolute terms against listed reseller peers Crayon (CRAYON.OL), Softcat (SCT.L), Computacenter (CCC.L), CDW, and Insight (NSIT). The earnings-power-value margin of safety of +58% is a positive signal. But the near-zero reported net margin (0.1%) is materially weaker than Softcat (roughly 5-6% net) and Computacenter (roughly 3-4% net), so the discount is partly earned quality, not pure mispricing - the cheapness is on free cash flow and operating earnings, not on net margin. With no peer override or anchor multiple supplied, the operating multiple is anchored at a discount to the group (about 6-9 times enterprise value to adjusted operating earnings, versus richer peers) rather than crediting the full gap as upside. This is a single-name, company-specific post-merger re-rating, not sector-wide regulatory beta, so the discount to peers is creditable as name-specific edge to the extent the savings convert the margin gap - but it should be quality-haircut.

# A deep dive on management and the setup - SoftwareOne Holding AG (SWON.SW)

*Sector: Technology (IT lifecycle / software-and-cloud reseller-aggregator) - Signal: deep value - Transcript anchor: full-year 2025 results call, 2026-03-31*

---

## 1. The narrative arc

The single transcript provided (full-year 2025, held 2026-03-31) is a post-merger 'we delivered, now we accelerate' story. The core arc is the transformation from a stand-alone Swiss software-licensing reseller into a combined SoftwareOne and Crayon 'global software and cloud leader' - CHF 14bn in combined gross sales, 70,000 clients, 13,000 colleagues, 70-plus countries. The narrative has three load-bearing pillars, each introduced in this call:

1. **Return to growth after a self-inflicted air-pocket.** Management frames 2025 as 'transformational' and stresses the trajectory: full-year like-for-like revenue (organic growth excluding the merger) of +1.4% (versus 'broadly flat' guidance) accelerating to +11% in the fourth quarter. The story is explicitly about cadence - 'growth improved steadily throughout 2025, and by Q3, we were back to positive territory.'
2. **Cost savings as the margin engine.** A CHF 43m annual run-rate by end-2025, CHF 64m 'as of today' (March 2026), committed to CHF 80-100m. This is the quantified bridge from a 20.9% adjusted operating margin toward the above-23% they now target.
3. **AI and multi-vendor as the new secular tailwind.** The Microsoft incentive-program headwinds (the 2024-25 drag) are declared over ('we do not see any headwinds related to the EA incentives' in 2026); the baton passes to the cloud-solution-provider transition, the first global authorized distributor role for Google Cloud (February 2026, 10 markets), and AI consulting and cost-management demand - plus an explicit Anthropic/OpenAI 'we help customers choose models' reselling angle.

What was quietly dropped or restructured: the old 'rest of Europe' reporting is gone, re-cut into Nordics / Western Europe / Central and Eastern Europe - a reorganization that conveniently resets the comparison base right as Crayon's Nordic-heavy book is consolidated. North America (down 12.6%) and Latin America (down 4.4%) are the narrative's problem children, handled with 'turnaround gaining traction' and an outright exit from 4 Latin American countries (Argentina, Uruguay, El Salvador, Nicaragua) - that is, portfolio pruning rather than an organic fix.

Because only one quarter's transcript is in evidence, the evolution of management thinking across quarters cannot be traced directly; the arc is reconstructed from the multi-year financial trajectory plus this single call. I flag that explicitly rather than fabricate prior-quarter quotes.

## 2. Claims versus the financials (forensic)

| Management claim (full-year 2025 call) | Hard evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| '2025 was transformational... returned to growth, +1.4% like-for-like, +11% in Q4' | Reported revenue grew +22.5% year over year (CHF 1.02bn to CHF 1.24bn) - but that is the reported number, inflated by six months of Crayon consolidation (the deal closed 2 July 2025). The honest organic figure is the +1.4% like-for-like that management itself cites. | Partially supported but optically inflated. The headline +22.5% is merger arithmetic, not organic. The real organic story is low-single-digit, back-loaded into the fourth quarter. |
| 'Profitability remained strong - adjusted operating margin 20.9%, up 0.5pp' | Reported net margin is 0.1% (net income of CHF 0.9m on CHF 1.24bn). Earnings per share of CHF 0.0059. Gross margin 44.1% but flagged as contracting. The gulf between 20.9% adjusted operating margin and roughly 0% net margin is the entire forensic story: depreciation and amortization on the Crayon purchase-price allocation, integration charges, and interest swallow the adjusted profit. | The gap between adjusted and reported is enormous. 'Strong profitability' is true only on the adjusted line; on a reported basis the company is at breakeven. The finance chief admits the goal is merely to 'narrow the gap' to under CHF 30m of adjustments (excluding Crayon costs). |
| Savings: CHF 64m run-rate, committed CHF 80-100m | Consistent internally and corroborated by post-call disclosure (CHF 80m-plus realized by the first quarter of 2026, per web verification). Free cash flow supports it: trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of CHF 228m, up about 702% year over year, at 5.5 times free cash flow. | Credible and the strongest leg. The cash is real and the savings math is delivering. This is where the value is. |
| 'No further impairments expected in 2026... would have impaired already' (finance chief) | Only Latin America was written down (CHF 8m) in 2025. But five-year net income is a roller-coaster: +CHF 118m (2021), -CHF 58m (2022), +CHF 21m, -CHF 2m, +CHF 0.9m. Altman Z-score of 0.6 (bankruptcy-risk zone), Piotroski 3 out of 9. | Assertive but unproven. The balance-sheet stress scores undercut the 'no impairments' confidence. The purchase-price value on a CHF 1bn-plus Crayon deal is a live write-down risk if North America and Latin America stay weak. |
| 'Net cash' implied by capital-allocation comfort | Live balance-sheet recompute: net cash of about CHF 360m (cash CHF 434m versus debt CHF 74m). | Confirmed - and the single best fact in the file. This is a genuine sum-of-the-parts add-back and the floor under the thesis. |
| Microsoft incentive headwind 'over'; price increases 'help our business' | The company has beaten earnings expectations in 0 of 7 quarters (0%); consensus forward earnings-per-share growth is +29.8%, forward revenue growth +29.8%. The headwind being over is an assertion about 2026, untestable today. | Watch closely. A management that has beaten 0 of 7 quarters declaring the headwind cleanly behind it deserves skepticism, not the benefit of the doubt. |

**Net:** the narrative is richer than the reported trajectory justifies on profitability (the adjusted gloss over a roughly 0% net margin) but poorer than the cash generation justifies on quality (trailing free cash flow of CHF 228m and net cash of CHF 360m are genuinely strong and under-narrated relative to the margin spin).

## 3. Tone and confidence trajectory

**Within the one call: rising and offensively postured.** The language is forward-leaning and growth-coded - 'transformational,' 'we have delivered,' 'stronger position to drive momentum,' 'scale matters.' The Co-CEO volunteers the Anthropic/OpenAI angle unprompted, a tell that management wants to be associated with the AI narrative. The finance chief is notably confident on North America ('I'm very confident... we will return into growth') and on impairments ('no further impairments expected').

Across the multi-year financials, however, the implied trajectory is declining-then-stabilizing: a 2021 peak (net income CHF 118m, earnings per share CHF 0.76), a 2022 collapse (-CHF 58m), and a 2023-25 grind near breakeven, now pivoting on the Crayon merger. So the tone is rising while the delivered earnings-per-share base has compounded at -70.3% over five years. That divergence is the thing to watch.

No defensive or macro-hedging language dominates - this is an offensive call. The only defensive notes are the Latin American country exits and the careful 'narrow the gap' framing on adjustments.

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Reliability: medium (skewed low on earnings per share, fair on revenue cadence).**
- A beat rate of 0 of 7 quarters (0%) on earnings per share is the dominant credibility fact and it is poor. Whatever the adjusted narrative, the company has not delivered a single earnings beat in the sample.
- Against that: full-year 2025 like-for-like revenue (+1.4%) came in ahead of the 'broadly flat' guidance - a genuine, if modest, beat on the top line. The fourth-quarter +11% inflection is real.
- The savings guidance (CHF 80-100m) is being delivered ahead of schedule (CHF 64m at the call, CHF 80m-plus by the first quarter of 2026 per web verification), which is the most credible guidance in the file.
- No clear sandbagging pattern; if anything the earnings misses suggest the opposite (optimistic reported-earnings framing).

Net: trust the revenue-cadence and savings guidance; discount the path-to-reported-profit guidance heavily given 0 of 7.

## 5. Analyst pressure points

Analysts (Kepler, UBS, Berenberg, Finanz und Wirtschaft) pushed on exactly the soft spots:
- **Microsoft incentive changes** (Treisch, Kepler) - the recurring wound. Management's answer ('no headwinds in 2026, possibly stabilization or tailwind') is asserted, and given 0 of 7 beats, this is the number-one thing that could turn into bad news.
- **One-off charges and adjustments in 2026** (Tong, UBS) - analysts are clearly worried the adjusted operating earnings keep leaking into reported. The finance chief's 'under CHF 30m, excluding Crayon' is a deflection-by-carve-out: the Crayon integration cost (the big number) is explicitly excluded from the cap.
- **North America turnaround timing** (Burgi) - pressed for a date; got 'improvement already in Q1 versus Q4' but no hard milestone.
- **Impairment risk** (Wolf, Berenberg) - directly asked if more write-downs could come in 2026; the finance chief's 'no' is hostage to North America and Latin America performance.
- **Capital-spending guidance** (Bader) - a non-answer ('similar level'), mild.

The pattern: management is most evasive precisely where the gap between reported and adjusted earnings lives (one-off charges, impairments) and most confident where it is least testable (the 2026 incentive tailwind).

## 6. Red flags and green flags

Red flag: **earnings beat rate 0 of 7 (0%)** - chronic under-delivery versus the analyst bar (full-year 2025 call context).
Red flag: **reported net margin 0.1% / earnings per share CHF 0.0059** against a 20.9% adjusted operating margin - the adjustment bridge is doing all the work; five-year earnings-per-share growth -70.3% (multi-year trajectory).
Red flag: **Altman Z-score 0.6 (bankruptcy-risk band) plus Piotroski 3 out of 9** - weak solvency and quality scores despite the net-cash position (driven by thin reported earnings and the leveraged-looking accounting picture after the purchase-price allocation).
Red flag: **gross margin 'contracting' (44.1%)** - structural pressure on the reseller's cut; Microsoft controls the incentive pool.
Red flag: **'adjustments cap excludes Crayon integration cost'** - the carve-out where the real cash leakage hides (finance chief, in the Q&A).
Red flag: **smart-money score 9 out of 100** - institutional conviction is essentially absent.

Green flag: **net cash of about CHF 360m** (cash CHF 434m versus debt CHF 74m) - a hard, verifiable balance-sheet floor and a real sum-of-the-parts add-back.
Green flag: **trailing free cash flow CHF 228m, up about 702% year over year; 5.5 times free cash flow** - the cash engine is real and cheap; free-cash-flow margin 16.3%.
Green flag: **savings ahead of plan** (CHF 64m to CHF 80m-plus, target CHF 100m) - the margin-expansion path is being executed, not just promised (corroborated to the first quarter of 2026 via web verification).
Green flag: **fourth-quarter +11% revenue inflection plus first global Google Cloud distributor** - genuine top-line re-acceleration and a new channel lane.
Green flag: **2026 guidance lifted at the first-quarter-2026 call** to mid-to-high-single-digit constant-currency growth and an adjusted operating margin above 23% (web-verified) - management raising, not cutting.

## 7. Hidden signals

- **The segment re-cut (rest-of-Europe into Nordics / Western Europe / Central and Eastern Europe) resets the comparison base** exactly as Crayon consolidates - convenient, and it makes organic-versus-acquired hard to disentangle from the outside. Watch for the first clean combined-organic comparison in 2026.
- **'Adjusted operating earnings' is the unit of account in every positive sentence; 'reported' figures only appear when the finance chief speaks.** A deliberate framing split between the growth narrators (the Co-CEOs) and the numbers person (finance chief Schraner). That split is itself the signal: the equity story lives on adjusted, the truth lives on reported.
- **The Crayon receivables overhang is quietly closing** - the Philippines public-sector receivable (USD 22m collected, the rest 'shortly') is management closing a Crayon legacy problem, a small green flag on integration hygiene.
- **AI is positioned as a complexity tailwind, not a threat** - 'our business model really thrives around complexity... we help guide customers which model makes sense.' This is the bullish framing of the same force (generative AI) that is a terminal threat to low-switching-cost resellers. Management is on the right side of it for now (multi-vendor brokerage is harder to bypass than pure license resale), but it is a service, not a competitive moat.
- **Capital allocation went all-in on Crayon; no buyback or dividend signal** despite net cash - the entire balance sheet is committed to integration, so the trigger for value realization is operational (margin delivery), not a return-of-capital event.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: turning (toward stable if savings land).** This is a company mid-pivot from a stalled stand-alone reseller into a consolidated scale-play, with the Crayon deal as the axis. The pivot is executing - savings ahead of plan, fourth-quarter inflection, lifted 2026 guidance - but it is not yet proven on the reported line (0 of 7 beats, roughly 0% net margin, bankruptcy-risk-band Altman Z-score).

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** is the gap between the 20.9% adjusted operating margin and the roughly 0% reported net margin a transient integration artifact that closes as savings hit and purchase-price and integration charges roll off - or a permanent feature of a structurally low-margin, Microsoft-incentive-dependent reseller? If transient, this is a 5.5-times-free-cash-flow, net-cash compounder re-rating toward peers (Softcat and Computacenter trade far richer). If permanent, the optical cheapness is a value trap on a base where the reseller's cut is being compressed. The net cash (CHF 360m) is what makes the downside survivable while that question resolves.

**Competitive edge.** Type: intangible / scale-cost plus switching-cost (weak) - vendor authorizations (now including the global Google Cloud distributor role), 70,000 client relationships, multi-vendor compliance know-how. Evidence read: return on invested capital of 10.7% is positive, but return on equity is only 1.8% and gross margin is contracting at 44.1%. A cut that the platform vendors (Microsoft) can move at will is the opposite of pricing power. This is a narrow edge - real but shallow, resting on brokerage complexity rather than hard lock-in. Trend: stable (scale from Crayon offsets the squeeze on the cut; the Google Cloud distributor win argues for mild widening of the vendor-breadth edge, but gross-margin contraction argues against calling it a widening one).

**Secular threat.** The relevant force is generative-AI displacement and cheaper-labor deflation of IT-services and software brokerage. Verdict: manageable, not terminal. Pure license-resale revenue per client is exposed, but the business is migrating toward the cloud-solution-provider model, cloud migration services, cost management, and AI-model brokerage - exactly the 'manage complexity across many vendors' niche that is harder to bypass than horizontal software. AI is currently a tailwind to the services book (consulting and migration demand) even as it pressures the legacy license cut. The cheapness is therefore better read as a fear-print from a self-inflicted air-pocket being repaired by a real merger, not a structurally melting base - provided the savings and margin bridge actually closes the reported-versus-adjusted gap. The net-cash balance sheet means this is not a solvency-distress value trap even if the re-rating is slow.

Credibility: 3 out of 5. Trajectory: turning. Competitive edge: narrow. Edge trend: stable. Secular threat: manageable.
